Interesting that Orthodoxy is making a comeback (arrival?) in the US and all over the world lately. Not exactly a cultural revolution, but it's growing at a lively pace. And I'm not surprised. They have exactly the message that appeals to people who reject all this modern nonsense, consumerism, immorality, sloth, greed, shallowness and vanity, lust and deviancy. It's basically a code to live a simple life, reject modernity, embrace the traditional values, venerate your ancestors and their traditions and wisdom, eat moderately and healthy, exercise, have a family, cut all the media and brain rotting garbage out of your life, learn self denial and restraint, basically learn to let the world flow around you without moving you or shaking you. I'm not really religious, but I can definitely see how this all appeals very much to a whole class of mostly younger people who are looking for something meaningful, some direction, an anchor for their lives and beings. Nothing against the Catholic Church but they haven't stood fast in most western nations and they don't seem as appealing somehow, just another weak reed. Not saying that is the case, but people fear that they will be let down. 
But yes, you are right. And thank God for that. Also interesting that there are actually more Christians in Africa today than in the US (or maybe that was including Asia), and it's by far the fastest growth area. It's just Protestantism (and I suspect a fair number of them are only very loosely "Christian" from what I have seen) but it's an interesting fact. replies.author: @db1machine
replies.text:
@ I had the fortunate experience of a childhood schooling in a small rural English village. All good fiction is rooted somewhat in reality. We wore house color uniforms, black, gold and red. Huge willows lined the walls and battlements of the gothic building by the river. Not to mention that our logo for the Sir John Gleed was literally a sword and armor. There is a lot more to those stories for those that grew up there. While fantastic fiction may expand these stories, at their core, it requires less imagination when you’ve grown up around the land that made such fairy tales. My problem lies with the injection of a virtuous agenda where it is not needed. Lessons learned by these stories that are human in nature, not specific to any particular demographic. 

1998 was the first time I read Harry Potter. And it gave me just a hint that the golden street lamps lining my road, would go out along the greenery of the lanes and a wizard might just pop out to tell me I’m special. At its core, isn’t that every 11 year olds wish? No matter who they are, or how they love? I like to think so.

replies.author: @Owlr4ider
replies.text:
@johnnyjack4079 Any time it genuinely fits the story, it's that simple. For example in a movie like Black Panther(and its sequel) it makes perfect for the majority of the cast to be black, though there were certainly too many main female characters in them due to DEI, especially in the sequel. If anything the white CIA agent was very out of place in Black Panther 1 and was basically the token white guy on the set, a position usually reserved to black people in an otherwise all white cast. Similarly if you write a story taking place in say China than having the cast predominantly Chinese, or at the very least Asian, makes perfect sense.

Similarly if you write a story happening in America but isn't periodic/historical/etc but say modern day than yes make it as diverse as modern day America is, again as long as it fits the story you're trying to tell. Just don't put random black people in a story taking place in ancient Rome and gas light your audience saying 'black people existed in ancient Rome' or some other idiotic line. I mean sure, they did exist in ancient Rome, mostly as slaves(sad but true, I know) but none of them were historically relevant to the point where their inclusion in any story taking in that setting makes no sense whatsoever.
